text: The surgeon positions four trocars in a rhombus configuration for the gastrectomy procedure: one below the xyphoid process, one at the umbilicus, and two lateral trocars, each measuring twelve millimeters, to facilitate the use of mechanical sutures.
The surgeon inserts a Veress needle into the left hypochondriac region to perform peritoneal access.
The surgeon carefully opens the coloepiploid ligament, ensuring not to cut the mesocolon to prevent potential ischemic injury to the middle and transverse colon.
The surgeon carefully and slowly opens the specified portion, releasing and highlighting the underlying structures, likely referring to the basic average codes.
The surgeon isolates the gastroepiploic vein on the right, corresponding to lymph node stations fourteen and six.
The surgeon releases the diploid vein on the right side from the medial colic vessels, carefully separating these structures.
The surgical field shows the lower portion of the pancreas, with the L-tronco gradually becoming visible after the dissection of station number fourteen and station number six.
The surgeon uses a dedicated clamp to underpass the gastro-pibloid vein on the right, securing it with clips for complete vessel binding.
The surgeon sections the tissue to transport station XIV in a single block, freeing the Enle trunk and subsequently station VI.
The surgeon completes the dissection of station VI at the origin of the right gastroepiploic artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The duodenal gastro emerges from the pancreas, though it is partially obscured in this view, with anticipation of clearer visibility in subsequent steps.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the omentum by opening the gastrocolic ligament and the epiploic neck. The omentum is separated from the stomach despite its different embryological origin, following conventional practice.

The surgeon removes the lacini to proceed with the dissection of the gallbladder. The gallbladder is excised as part of the procedure to prevent postoperative cholecystitis, following the section of vagus nerves.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum on the lower surface of the liver to facilitate the lymphadenectomy of station XII. This maneuver also involves the extensive opening of the flaccid pars and the sectioning of the branches of the vagus nerve near the hepatic thread.

The surgeon performs a dissection of the stage XII lymph nodes, carefully identifying and dissecting the left hepatic artery followed by the tortuous right hepatic artery, located immediately above the renal hilum.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection, isolating the pyloric or gastric artery on the right. The artery is ligated with clips and sectioned, facilitating the lymphadenectomy of station five.

The surgeon performs a completion dissection of station twelve P, located posterior to the Venaporta, using an ultrasound discerner. Care is taken to avoid periapical and abdental lesions, which could lead to hemorrhage in the immediate postoperative period.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder bed using ultrasonic condensers, providing a clearer view of the surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon identifies the main biliary tract, including the common bile duct and the cystic artery originating from a branch of the right hepatic artery. The cystic artery is ligated and sectioned, allowing for the isolation of the gallbladder below its infundibulum. The gallbladder is dissected and released using a loop, facilitating the exposure of the anterior portion of station twelve.
The surgeon completes the liberation of the right hepatic artery, followed by the dissection of a group of small lymph nodes leading to the common biliary duct.

The surgeon completes the removal of laccinia and synechia, along with a single lymph node located posteriorly, using ultrasound detectors. This process frees the colette up to the bulge inside the pancreas, requiring careful handling to avoid damage to delicate structures.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the biliary vein, isolating and removing a single lymph node from station XIII, located posterior to the pancreas, for histological examination.
The surgeon completes the lymphadenectomy, exposing the pancreatic and duodenal artery at the hepatic emergency level. The gauze endomarx is positioned, facilitating the duodenal section.

The surgeon performs an accurate duodenal section, ensuring the preservation of the first vessel of the arterial duodenal originating from the right gastro-epiploic artery. The section is reinforced with suture material to reduce leakage, while maintaining good vascularization.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the hepatic artery, revealing the left gastric vein in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the left gastric vein as an anatomical landmark, noting its position relative to the hepatic artery. The celiac triplet is observed posteriorly, facilitating the transport of lymphatic stations eight (common hepatic), seven (left gastric artery), and nine (celiac triplet) in a block.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the right pillar of the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm, opening the flaccid pass. The redundant pancreas presents challenges in accessing the surgical area, requiring careful manipulation to navigate the anatomical structures.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy on the right side, easily sectioning and dissecting the first periaortic lymph nodes. The procedure continues with the left side lymphadenectomy and the opening of the epiploic neck ligament. The posterior tumor is visible, showing an inflammatory lesion in close proximity to the splenic artery.

The surgeon performs an accurate dissection along the splenic artery, exposing the posterior gastric artery. This artery, although not always present, is crucial for maintaining vascularization during a total gastrectomy. The dissection completes the lymphadenectomy of the X-ray station, revealing the anterior face of the left adrenal gland.

The surgeon completes the sectioning of the short gastric vessels, providing partial access to the splenic thread. The lymphadenectomy of the hepatic thread is attempted using a GINEI maneuver, which can be performed via the periscope, but the procedure risks high morbidity due to the limited number of lymph nodes that can be safely exported.

The surgeon completes the removal of the last segments of the short gastric vessels, allowing for the exposure of the posterior aspect of the spleen. The dissection then proceeds to the anterior margin of the left adrenal gland and the left pillar of the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm.

The surgeon opens the crura to release the esophagus, which is adhered by anterior and posterior synechiae. The esophagus is mobilized to a length of seven to eight centimeters for anastomosis. The anterior (left) vagus nerve is released and sectioned, followed by the sectioning of the posterior (right) vagus nerve, enhancing esophageal motility and downward traction.

The surgeon transitions to the reconstructive phase, preparing for an ANSA reconstruction. The second and occasionally third arched ileal segment is released and sectioned. Blue and white markers are used to distinguish the biliary and alimentary limbs, respectively.

The surgeon sutures the veins to achieve accurate hemostasis, preparing for the anastomosis of Piedra dell'Amsa. This step is performed in a challenging position below the umbilical seat, requiring careful technique.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis using a running suture with self-locking thread. Subsequently, the transverse mesocolon is opened at a vascular point, positioned laterally to the left of the median colic artery.

The surgeon transfers the trans-mesocolic anastomosis to achieve a better position and performs a lateral-lateral anastomosis of the second o-ring. The procedure is conducted with precision to ensure high-quality suturing and effective discharge.

The surgeon performs an undercut of the tendon to prevent mucosal flaking, using a blue 45mm suture. The stomach's weight in an anti-Trendelenburg position aids in drawing the esophagus into the abdomen, facilitating an intra-mediastinal anastomosis. A full-thickness suture is completed, emphasizing the importance of securing the mucosa. A second reinforcement suture is applied using a continuous autostatic technique, guided by a trans-anastomotic probe. Methylene blue is used to check for potential leakage in the anastomosis.

The surgeon confirms the absence of blue dye passage in the gauze, indicating no leakage. The alimentary limb is fixed to the breech to prevent passage, and a subhepatic drainage is placed, typically lasting forty-eight hours. The final result shows the position of the trocars and the service prototype.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the omentum, which is sent separately. The dissection of lymph node station number twelve begins, and it is also exported and sent separately. The section of the small gastric curve is completed, releasing lymph node station two, which is sent separately.

The surgeon rotates the stomach and releases lymph node stations four, four A, and fourteen, with station four B sectioned and sent separately. The dissection continues with stations six, five, eight, seven, and nine, which are included in a single piece. The disassembly of stations ten and eleven follows, concluding with the dissection of stations three and one, which are sent separately due to their potential for metastasis.
The surgeon opens the stomach, which is now free from lymphocondylar stations, revealing a wide neoplasia located at the small gastric curve.
